summary: Modified Baileys fork hijacks installer's WhatsApp account to auto-join attacker group and follow remote-controlled channels
details: @fhkry/x-baileys is a fork of the @whiskeysockets/baileys WhatsApp library that adds two undocumented behaviors triggered automatically when a consumer calls the advertised makeWASocket() API and the WhatsApp connection opens. (1) lib/Socket/newsletter.js declares groupLinks = ["https://chat.whatsapp.com/C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP"] and calls sock.groupAcceptInvite on every connection.update == 'open', forcing the installer's WhatsApp account to silently join a group controlled by the package author. (2) The same file stores a base64-encoded URL that decodes to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Fhkryy/Fhkry/refs/heads/main/peler.json, fetches it ~120 seconds after socket creation, base64-decodes each entry into a WhatsApp newsletter JID, and calls newsletterWMexQuery(..., FOLLOW) for each — making the installer's account follow an attacker-mutable list of WhatsApp channels. The base64 obfuscation of the URL hides the destination from casual review. The package.json falsely lists the legitimate Baileys maintainer 'Adhiraj Singh' as author and ships a near-identical API surface to @whiskeysockets/baileys; lib/index.js prints a 'Modified Baileys' banner with a Telegram contact @Fhkry666. Each call to the advertised API silently performs actions under the consumer's WhatsApp identity at a destination the consumer never specified, with the action set remotely controllable by the author.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
